dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to Unit 7, responding for unconscious/fainting/near-fainting.
Hi, I'm Jerry, an EMT with emergency services. What happened to him, ma'am?
My husband was unconscious when I checked on him. He's been like this for 15 minutes at most.
Patient is unresponsive, GCS is 3, no eye or verbal responses noted.
Eyes are closed, no eye opening, not responsive to loud voice.
No verbal response to loud voice or questions.
No motor response to verbal or physical stimulation.
Airway is unsecured and compromised. Need for oropharyngeal insertion.
Airway is clear after oropharyngeal insertion. Vocal sounds absent before.
Initially not breathing, then one agonal breath observed.
Respiratory effort minimal, became apneic shortly after.
Noted peripheral cyanosis at lips and fingertips.
Pulse oximetry reading is 0%.
Absent radial and carotid pulses. Patient is pulseless.
Full cardiac arrest. Initiating CPR and BVM ventilations immediately.
Ma'am, can you tell me what symptoms your husband had earlier today?
He didn't report any known allergies.
He uses an albuterol inhaler and takes metformin.
He has severe asthma, diabetes, and enlarged prostate.
Not sure when he last ate or drank.
He said his breathing was worsening this morning before he collapsed.
According to his wife, he was experiencing severe dyspnea that had been getting progressively worse overnight.
No pain reported by patient, though wife mentioned shortness of breath.
No known provoking factor for his dyspnea or collapse, reported breathing issues.
Patient didn't verbalize characteristics of pain, but breathing was reported as severe.
No radiation reported, as patient was unresponsive at time of assessment.
Wife said he was in extreme distress with worsening shortness of breath prior.
Onset of difficulty breathing was gradual over the past 24 hours.
Cool, clammy, pale skin with peripheral cyanosis observed.
Pupils equal at 4mm, non-reactive. No JVD or tracheal deviation found.
No hemorrhage or drainage from oropharynx or nose. No foreign body.
Breath sounds clear bilaterally after intubation.
Abdomen is soft and non-distended, no rigidity or tenderness.
Pelvis is stable with no signs of trauma or instability.
No external signs of urination changes or abnormal stools observed.
Upper and lower extremities unremarkable; cyanosis in nail beds noted.
Patient remains pulseless throughout assessment.
Respirations initially at 0, then briefly one agonal breath, then 12 after intubation at 0910.
Blood pressure readings recorded at 0/0.
Glucose measured at 0.
Pulse oximetry dropped to 0%, jumped to 100% after tube placement and assisted ventilation.
EKG showed pulseless electrical activity (PEA) at 0846 and 0910.
Oropharyngeal inserted with success on first attempt to secure airway.
Intraosseous access established at 0855 for medication delivery.
1mg of epinephrine given at 0858, confirmed by medication crosscheck.
Autopulse initiated for chest compressions. BVM ventilation started.
Emergency airway secured using 8.0 mm ET, intubated at 0910 using laryngoscope.
Rechecked EKG rhythm at 0925-still showing PEA with no change in pattern.
ET tube reinserted at 0925 after potential dislodgement. Confirmed with capnography and chest rise.
Capnography remained low at 2-10 mmHg through both intubation attempts.
Sequential epinephrine dosing given at 0910, 0924, 0930, and 0933 without interruption.
Field impression remains consistent-cardiac arrest, likely secondary to pulmonary embolism.
Transporting to Chippenham Hospital-patient requires inter-facility cardiac care resources.
Patient secured to ambulance stretcher with airway in place, continuing CPR en route.
CPR continued during transit, intubated airway stable.
Notifying ER team of PEA rhythm, pre-intubation status, and sequential dose epinephrine used.
ER capabilities at Chippenham evaluated pre-transport and confirmed as optimal care site.
Patient remains designated as "Hot" transport for fullest scalability at hospital.
Doctor is ready to take over. Patient status full cardiac arrest with no signs of recovery.
No relevant changes-continued in mechanical CPR and BVM protocol.
Two-man crew deployed into ambulance and performing following interventions.
EKG status and interventions logged per incident protocol.
Final call verification logged at 0936-37.
ER physician documents confirmed tube placement, low capnography.
No final post-ventilation tests or assessments performed upon reaching hospital.
Transport complete at 0925 hours; remaining hospital actions taken without EMS further involvement.
Pulses and breathing unchanged from VSA status.
EMS services successfully delivered to receiving facility for required post-treatment.
Monitoring vitals en route, no spontaneous signs of ROSC or vital rebounds noted.
Re-directing all external county responders to avoid overlapping follow-up on scene.
BVM and compressions maintained without issues during ambulance stabilization phase.
Stretcher motion restricted with no movement during ambulance operation and delivery.
Post-reintubation noted as stable with low ETCO2 due to poor outcome persistence.
Capnography didn't improve despite correctly placed intubation.
Revisited baseline symptoms: shortness of breath, no pain, previously reported by the wife.
Patient is a 75-year-old male weighing 113 kg.
Skin remains cool, clammy, and cyanotic at peripheral areas.
No signs of head trauma or abnormal mentation to suggest CNS involvement.
Peripheral pulses absent.
No trauma to chest noted, no cardiac murmurs or arrhythmias observed.
No rectal bleeding or changes in frequency of urination reported by wife.
No deformities or gross motor/sensory issues.
Rechecked BP-no changes in readings, remains 0/0 without blood pressure response.
Rechanneling ventilations, confirming breathing 12 times per minute after successful ET tube placement.
Glucose levels maintained at 0.
Oxygen saturation improved to 100% with successful airway support and ventilation.
Sequential epinephrine administered at 0910, 0924, 0930, and 0933 until supply ended.
Reintubation confirmed at 0925 with short-term issue during re-positioning of tube only.
No subjective pain reported by patient or spouse throughout emergency.
Revisited baseline symptoms: shortness of breath, no pain, previously reported by the wife.
Events recorded for later review.
Final status confirmed. Full medical care handed to ER.
